rights.basis: A 1977 scholarly edition by a modern editor is not presumed public domain, and no open license was found in the consulted catalog evidence.

bibliographic.citation: R. M. Wilson, ed., Sawles Warde: An Early Middle English Homily, Edited from the Bodley, Royal and Cotton MSS. (Leeds: School of English Language, University of Leeds, 1938), Leeds School of English Language Texts and Monographs 3–4, xlvi + 115 pages.
bibliographic.publisher: School of English Language, University of Leeds
bibliographic.series: Leeds School of English Language Texts and Monographs 3–4
bibliographic.title: Sawles Warde: An Early Middle English Homily, Edited from the Bodley, Royal and Cotton MSS.

entities.description: R. M. Wilson's three-witness diplomatic edition with introduction, notes, glossary, parallel witness texts, the then-assumed Latin source, and a reconstructed stemma. The inherited U-M header names it as source, but the electronic stream does not carry Wilson's pagination.
entities.label: Wilson, Sawles Warde, 1938
notes: Wilson's attribution of the Latin source to Hugh of Saint-Victor's De anima is historically important but superseded by the identification of the pseudo-Anselmian De custodia interioris hominis.

rights.basis: The 1938 publication date alone does not establish public-domain status in every jurisdiction, and renewal/publication facts were not fully researched.
rights.component: Wilson's modern editorial matter, introduction, reconstructed text, notes, and glossary

access.format: Reference-quality PDF scans subject to legal and conservation review

entities.description: The principal Bodley witness of the Katherine Group. Sawles Warde occupies ff. 72r–80v and is imperfect because two leaves are missing after fol. 80.

holding.notes: Sawles Warde ff. 72r–80v; consult in the Weston Library.

entities.label: Bodleian Library MS Bodley 34

notes: Two leaves are missing after fol. 80, so this witness breaks off before the homily's end.
The official catalog describes South-West Midlands AB language and only tentatively associates the manuscript with Hereford.

entities.description: Composite parchment codex whose first-half-thirteenth-century West Midlands part contains Sawles Warde at ff. 1r–10v. Fol. 10v preserves Johan's scribal colophon and a nine-line rhymed prayer after the homily.

holding.notes: Sawles Warde ff. 1r–10v; physical consultation is in London and requires a letter of introduction.

entities.label: British Library Royal MS 17 A XXVII

notes: The codex also contains later fifteenth-century prayers and hymns at ff. 71–97.

dateStatements.value: First half of the thirteenth century

entities.description: Composite parchment codex whose first-half-thirteenth-century Middle English component at ff. 14–147 contains Sawles Warde at ff. 105v–112v.

holding.notes: Sawles Warde ff. 105v–112v; physical consultation is in London and requires a letter of introduction.

entities.label: British Library Cotton MS Titus D XVIII

physicalDescription.extent: 148 leaves in three parts; relevant component ff. 14–147; Sawles Warde ff. 105v–112v
physicalDescription.format: Relevant component written in two columns of 24–30 lines
notes: The whole codex contains material from the thirteenth through sixteenth centuries; the Sawles Warde component is specifically dated to the first half of the thirteenth century.

entities.description: Wilson's hypothetical lost intermediary from which he derived the Bodley and Royal branches. It is a reconstructed stemmatic node, not an observed physical manuscript.
entities.label: Reconstructed lost exemplar X

entities.description: Wilson's hypothetical lost archetype above Cotton and the reconstructed X branch. It is a critical reconstruction rather than an extant physical object.
entities.label: Reconstructed lost archetype Z

entities.description: The pseudo-Anselmian Latin dialogue or sermon freely translated and expanded in Sawles Warde. This entity represents the conceptual source work only: the exact recension and physical exemplar used by the Middle English adapter have not been identified. Wilson's 1938 attribution to Hugh of Saint-Victor's De anima is retained as a superseded historical claim, not as current source identification.
entities.label: Conceptual source work: De custodia interioris hominis
notes: This catalog description is not a manuscript witness, scholarly edition, or claim about a specific Latin carrier.

next_steps: Locate surviving CTILIB documentation or backups and identify the exact object seen by John Cooper on 8 August 1989.
Compare a physical copy of d'Ardenne 1977, pp. 165–186, character by character with the OTA deposit and U-M page boundaries.
Ask OTA and U-M whether migration or conversion logs survive for item 1323 / U-1323-A.
notes: The current evidence strongly favors d'Ardenne 1977 over Wilson 1938 as the print ancestor but does not document the full transfer chain.
openQuestions.question: What exact CTILIB object and print source produced the 1989 OTA deposit, and when were d'Ardenne's page milestones added?

next_steps: Ask the Bodleian whether any complete digital Bodley 34 surrogate exists beyond the controlled 1960 printed facsimile and whether complete-codex mediated copying is feasible.
Ask the British Library whether Royal's historically reported full coverage and Cotton's legacy coverage can be restored or supplied, and request canvas/page inventories before claiming completeness.
If ordering images, request complete codices rather than cropped Sawles Warde folios unless the task is explicitly work-only.
openQuestions.question: Can a complete public, auditable manuscript-image inventory be recovered for Bodley 34, Royal 17 A XXVII, or Cotton Titus D XVIII?

next_steps: Consult Becker's source studies and current research on the pseudo-Anselmian De moribus tradition.
Do not substitute the obsolete Hugh of Saint-Victor / De anima attribution or claim an extant physical Latin exemplar without textual evidence.
openQuestions.question: Which recension and physical exemplar of De custodia interioris hominis underlies Sawles Warde?

next_steps: Request item-specific provenance and rights clarification from U-M and OTA.
Separate reuse of public-domain medieval readings from reuse of modern editorial selections, encoding, metadata, and photography.
Do not infer that one provider's rights statement grants rights in every upstream layer.
openQuestions.question: How should the U-M No Copyright–US statement, OTA CC BY-NC-SA 3.0 license, inherited modern edition, and unresolved CTILIB encoding be reconciled for downstream reuse?

reviewNotes: Keep the medieval work, three manuscript witnesses, scholarly editions, electronic encodings, repository artifact, and modern facsimiles as separate entities.
Treat d'Ardenne 1977 as the probable electronic print source and Wilson 1938 as an inherited conflicting claim; do not flatten the two into certainty.
Treat Royal's Johan colophon and nine-line prayer as witness-specific paratext retained by the electronic stream, not as proof that the anonymous homily is a mixed prose-and-verse work.
A complete printed edition that discusses or transcribes all three witnesses is not a complete manuscript facsimile. Manuscript-wide image access was checked separately for Bodley, Royal, and Cotton.
Underlying medieval text, modern editing, digital encoding, catalog prose, modern photography, and provider contract terms are distinct rights layers.

lineage.summary: The repository XML is a local copy or version of University of Michigan CME item Sawleswd, which carries OTA identifier U-1323-A. The current Oxford Text Archive item preserves John Cooper's 8 August 1989 deposit statement that the text was supplied ‘as seen on CTILIB.’ Although the U-M header names R. M. Wilson's 1938 edition as its source, the electronic page milestones 165–186 and direct text comparison instead strongly support Simonne d'Ardenne's 1977 Bodley-based Katherine Group edition as the probable print ancestor; the exact CTILIB conversion remains undocumented. The abstract medieval work is an early-thirteenth-century alliterative prose homily surviving in Bodley 34, Royal 17 A XXVII, and Cotton Titus D XVIII. The electronic stream appends Royal's scribal colophon and nine-line rhymed prayer after the homily; those are witness-specific paratext and do not make the abstract work mixed-form. Complete public Wilson, Huber–Robertson, and Kano sources and exact OTA, Helsinki, and official Bodleian catalog files are cached. No public complete manuscript-image set was found: Bodley has a complete 1960 photographic facsimile behind controlled/onsite access, Royal's catalog reports historical full coverage but exposes no live images, and Cotton's legacy complete-image route is explicitly unavailable.

catalogSummary.title: Sawles Warde

catalogingSubject.label: Sawles Warde
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The anonymous early-thirteenth-century Middle English prose homily Sawles Warde. The work is distinct from its three surviving manuscript witnesses, d'Ardenne's 1977 Bodley-based transcription, the 1989 OTA deposit, U-M's digital representation, and this repository's XML artifact. The checked-in representation also retains a Royal-witness scribal colophon and nine-line prayer after the homily; those witness-specific additions are separately scoped and do not make the abstract work mixed-form.

contentParts.label: Sawles Warde
notes: Bodley 34 is defective at the end; the electronic stream is therefore not a literal transcription of one extant manuscript throughout.
contentParts.summary: The complete allegorical homily, beginning with Matthew 24:43 / Luke 12:39 and concluding with a Trinitarian prayer and Amen.

contentParts.label: Royal scribal colophon
notes: Johan is treated as the Royal copyist named by the colophon, not as author of the abstract homily.
contentParts.summary: A witness-specific request to say a Paternoster for Johan, identified as the person who wrote the book.

contentParts.label: Royal nine-line prayer for the copyist

locators.value: BODY/P[29], nine verse lines flattened into one paragraph

contentParts.summary: A nine-line rhymed prayer asking readers to pray for the first-person speaker's soul.

assertion.notes: Tolkien dated the work to about 1210; Wilson proposed about 1210–15 or slightly earlier from linguistic evidence and explicitly called the result approximate. No numeric interval is normalized from those qualitative estimates.

notes: The XML header's bare 1240 belongs to inherited witness-era cataloging and must not be treated as an exact composition year.

assertion.notes: Bodley 34, Royal 17 A XXVII, and Cotton Titus D XVIII are all broadly assigned to the first half of the thirteenth century; the evidence does not establish which is earliest. Individual copy dates remain on the lineage witness entities.

dateStatements.display: Survives in three broadly first-half-thirteenth-century witnesses; their priority is unresolved
notes: No numeric interval is normalized from the shared qualitative dating.

extent.description: One prose homily followed by two Royal-witness additions. The checked-in XML BODY contains 1 HEAD, 29 P, 22 PB numbered 165–186, and no LG or L elements. P1–P27 encode the homily, P28 the Royal scribal colophon, and P29 a nine-line rhymed prayer flattened as prose markup. The XML omits d'Ardenne's opening Trinitarian invocation and the printed edition's front matter, back matter, and notes.

formStatements.description: Rhythmic, densely alliterative prose. The medieval work and surviving witnesses present the homily as prose rather than verse. The nine-line rhymed prayer is a Royal-witness scribal addition and is separately scoped; it does not make the abstract homily mixed-form.
A nine-line rhymed prayer following the Royal scribal colophon; the repository XML flattens it into one P element.

scope.description: The biblical incipit and brief scriptural formulae embedded in the Middle English homily

notes: The XML's page range 165–186 and text stream are evidence about an editorial/digital carrier, not manuscript foliation and not a medieval composition date.
Johan's colophon and prayer belong specifically to Royal MS 17 A XXVII; they are retained by the electronic stream but not generalized into abstract-work authorship or mixed form.
Rights, access, source conflicts, reconstructed lost exemplars, and complete-object facsimile findings are recorded in the paired lineage manifest.

next_steps: Locate surviving CTILIB documentation or backups and identify the exact display seen by John Cooper on 8 August 1989.
Compare a physical copy of d'Ardenne 1977, pages 165–186, character by character with the OTA deposit and U-M page boundaries.
Ask OTA and U-M whether migration or conversion logs survive for item 1323 / U-1323-A.
notes: The current evidence strongly favors d'Ardenne 1977 over Wilson 1938 as the print ancestor, but does not document the complete transfer chain.
openQuestions.question: What exact CTILIB object and print source produced the 1989 OTA deposit, and when were d'Ardenne's page milestones added?

next_steps: Review current AB-language localization work separately for composition, archetype, and each surviving witness.
Keep Herefordshire, anchoritic, and cloistered-nun claims qualified unless direct evidence narrows them.
openQuestions.question: Can Sawles Warde's composition site and audience be narrowed beyond a West Midlands work for a female contemplative or cloistered readership?

next_steps: Consult Becker's source studies and current work on the pseudo-Anselmian De moribus tradition.
Do not substitute the obsolete Hugh of Saint-Victor / De anima attribution or claim an extant physical exemplar without textual evidence.
openQuestions.question: Which recension and physical exemplar of De custodia interioris hominis underlies Sawles Warde?

assertion.notes: The broader Katherine/AB Group is associated with the Welsh Marches; this does not establish one exact composition site.

Wilson judged Herefordshire possible but not demonstrable; the current Bodleian catalog separately gives a low-certainty possible Hereford origin for Bodley 34. Witness provenance and scribal dialect are not converted into a secure work-composition claim.

notes: No named author is securely established. Similarity to other Katherine Group works does not prove common authorship.

assertion.notes: This records responsibility for a probable source edition, not authorship of the medieval work; the exact CTILIB-to-print transfer remains undocumented.

scope.description: The Bodley-based 1977 transcription that textual and pagination evidence identifies as the probable print ancestor of the electronic stream

assertion.notes: Wilson's editorial responsibility is certain, but the claim that his edition directly supplied the electronic text conflicts with the electronic pagination and d'Ardenne/Helsinki alignment.

scope.description: The 1938 three-manuscript edition named by the inherited U-M source description

subjects.label: Guardianship and salvation of the soul
Death, Hell, Heaven, fear, and divine love
Spiritual vigilance and defense against temptation

assertion.notes: Modern scholarship supports a female contemplative audience, but the exact institutional readership is not directly documented.

subjects.label: Female contemplative and anchoritic spiritual life

summaries.value: An anonymous early Middle English prose homily that imagines the body as a house guarding the soul. Reason governs the household; Will and the five senses require discipline; the four cardinal virtues defend the house; and messengers describing Hell and Heaven teach fear of damnation and love of eternal life. The work freely adapts the Latin De custodia interioris hominis into an expanded dramatic allegory.

The checked-in XML is one complete composite electronic reading, not three parallel manuscript transcriptions. It follows a Bodley-based edited stream through the damaged and lost end of Bodley 34, then preserves Royal readings and the Royal-only colophon and verse prayer. Its page milestones 165–186 align with d'Ardenne 1977, while its header instead claims Wilson 1938; that source conflict is retained in the paired lineage manifest.

assertion.notes: The title of J. R. R. Tolkien's modern-English translation, published in 2026; it is an alternate translated title, not the title of the U-M encoding.

Reason governs the household; Will and the five senses require discipline; the four cardinal virtues defend the house; and messengers describing Hell and Heaven teach fear of damnation and love of eternal life.
